coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots chelated with metal ions as efficient electrocatalysts for enhancing performance of microbial fuel cell based on sulfate reducing bacteria
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Loukanov, Alexandre
Angelov, Anatoliy
Takahashi, Yuki
Nikolov, Ivan
Nakabayashi, Seiichiro
coredata.dc:description: We report the electrocatalytic activity of a graphite electrode chemically coated with ultra-small nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped C-dots), which formed an intermediate diazo-metal chelate phase. Its performance as cathode for enhanced current production was demonstrated in a dual-chamber microbial fuel cell (MFC) through synergistic cooperation with electroactive biofilm of sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) on the anode. For that purpose, the immobilized SRB biofilm on the anode facilitated direct transfer of electrons to the graphite surface by filaments network, known as nano-pili, which provided cell-to-cell link. A significantly improved acceleration of the electrons was measured on the cathode in the presence of chemically conjugated N-doped C-dots chelated with metal ions, because they acted as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in a neutral phosphate buffer solution. As a result, the power density obtained from the occurred synergistic effect in MFC increased almost double higher than the initial value in the control experiment. The chemical resistance, exploitation life and the easy protocol for fabrication of the reported cathode coated with electrocatalytic nanolayer open up the possibility for new class cheap electrocatalytic electrodes with a wide practical applications in fuel cell engineering and technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Microbial fuel cell
ORR
Electroactive sulfate-reducing bacteria

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of N-doped C-dots
Fabrication of an electrocatalytic cathode coated with N-doped C-dots chelated with metal ions
Design of laboratory-scale proton exchange MFC with enhanced performance
Fabrication of eletrocatalytic cathode as a working electrode
Exploitation of the electrocatalytic cathode in MFC based on sulfate-reducing bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microscopic analysis and size distribution characterization of N-doped C-dots. (A) TEM image of well-dispersed spherical carbon nanoparticles (Scale bar = 10 nm). (B) Dynamic light scattering distribution of the nanoparticles dissolved in aqueous solution. (C) AFM topography image (upside) and their height profile (downside) of N-doped C-dots monolayer on mica substrate (Scale bar = 15 nm). On the topographic image upside is shown also an illustration model of the proposed chemical structure, height and spatial constitution of the polyaromatic carbon domains incorporated into the amorphous carbon network of the carbon nanoparticle, which is in agreement with AFM data.

Analysis and characterization of the chemical contents of the nanoparticles. (A) Raman, (B) normalized steady-state absorption (blue) and photoluminescence (red), (C) Fourier transformed infrared and (D) X-ray photoelectron scan spectra (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Fabrication of electrocatalytic cathode in two steps. First step, conjugation of N-doped C-dots on graphite surface through diazonium coupling reaction. Second step, formation of metal chelate complex as an electrocatalytic phase between the nanolayer and graphite surface.

Optical analysis of N-doped C-dots (CDs), nanoparticles conjugated to fluorescein through azo-bond (CDsNNAr) and metal chelate complex (CDsNNAr)Me2+. (A) absorbance (B) photoluminescence and (C) FTIR spectrum. With arrow is pointed IR band corresponded to diazo-group. At the bottom of the figure are shown images of the emission color of these three types nanoparticles in aqueous solution irradiated with conventional UV-lamp with 365 nm wavelength.

Cyclic voltammograms of the ORR electro-catalytic modified cathode. (A) Comparison of the ORR activity in saturated with oxygen phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) of C-dots modified graphite electrode contained Cu2+ (solid blue line), Fe2+ (solid brown line), Ru2+ (solid red line), bare graphite (solid gray line), graphite modified only with C-dots (solid violet line) as well as carbon-supported Pt catalyst (solid black line). (B) Comparison of the cathodic traces of working electrode modified with C-dots (solid violet line) with working electrode modified with C-dots and Ru2+ chelate complex in nitrogen (solid green line) and oxygen (solid red line) saturated solution (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Microbial fuel cell based on sulfate-reduction. (A) Laboratory-scale scheme of MFC including: 1 - feeding vessel, 2- feeding pump, 3- microbial fuel cell, 4- air, 5- electricity chain with a consumer, 6- buffer vessel, 7- recirculation pump, 8- collector tank. (B) Photographic image of MFC facility. (C) Scanning electron microscopy micrograph of a SRB colony formed on the graphite surface of the anode electrode. Scale bar = 1 μm.

Trends of power (A) and polarization (B) curves of a microbial fuel cell operating on sulfate-reduction (control experiment, blue line) and synergy between electroactive SRB biofilm on the anode electrode and electrocatalytic nanolayer immobilized on the cathode electrode (synergistic effect, red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots chelated with metal ions as efficient electrocatalysts for enhancing performance of microbial fuel cell based on sulfate reducing bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Strategic Research, Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Saitama University, Shimo-Ohkubo 255, Sakura-Ku, Saitama City 338-8570, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Engineering NanoBiotechnology, Department of Engineering Geoecology, University of Mining and Geology “St. Ivan Rilski”, Studentki Grad, “Prof. Boyan Kamenov” Str., Sofia 1700, Bulgaria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Mining and Geology “St. Ivan Rilski”

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Saitama University, Shimo-Ohkubo 255, Sakura-ku, Saitama-city 338-8570, Japan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the electrocatalytic activity of a graphite electrode chemically coated with ultra-small nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped C-dots), which formed an intermediate diazo-metal chelate phase. Its performance as cathode for enhanced current production was demonstrated in a dual-chamber microbial fuel cell (MFC) through synergistic cooperation with electroactive biofilm of sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) on the anode. For that purpose, the immobilized SRB biofilm on the anode facilitated direct transfer of electrons to the graphite surface by filaments network, known as nano-pili, which provided cell-to-cell link. A significantly improved acceleration of the electrons was measured on the cathode in the presence of chemically conjugated N-doped C-dots chelated with metal ions, because they acted as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in a neutral phosphate buffer solution. As a result, the power density obtained from the occurred synergistic effect in MFC increased almost double higher than the initial value in the control experiment. The chemical resistance, exploitation life and the easy protocol for fabrication of the reported cathode coated with electrocatalytic nanolayer open up the possibility for new class cheap electrocatalytic electrodes with a wide practical applications in fuel cell engineering and technology.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years the ecotechnologies based on microbial fuel cells (MFC) for treatment of wastewater, environmental pollutants and energy recovery were significantly improved in respect to the mild operation conditions as well as higher electric energy production [1–3]. An important progressive step was assigned to the ability of the oxidoreductase enzymes and electron transfer proteins of the microorganism communities in the electrocatalytic biofilm to conduct a direct electron transfer to the electrode surface in the anode chamber [4]. Since then, numerous improvements have been achieved including the establishment of rational ways for electrical interconnections between bacterial enzymes and electrodes [5]. Among them, special attention was given to the anaerobic sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) and introduction of these sulfidogenic microrganisms into the electroactive biofilm of MFC anode compartment based on their produced filaments known as nano-pili (D. desulfuricans, etc.) [6]. Nano-pili filaments induce stimulation of the biofilm growth and development around the metal or graphite electrode. They are electrically-conductive nanostructures [7] and enhanced the current production in MFC as a result of facilitated transfer of electrons from cell network to the anode surface. The mediation of electron transfer occurred directly via c-type cytochromes (soluble periplasmic proteins) associated with the outer membrane of SRB microorganisms [8]. The additional advantages of MFC associated with dissimilative microbial sulfate reduction are the low sludge separation and the economically removing of heavy metals from wastewater with simultaneous electricity production [9,10]. These characteristics and the ability of SRB to survive well in diverse environments [11] enable them the potential exploitation in bioelectrical engineered eco-friendly facilities at industrial level. Nevertheless, the poor cathodic reaction kinetics in the bioelectrochemical system and the consequent waste non-utilization as well as the energy losses restrict significantly the large-scale practical application of MFC [12]. Thus, a substantial improvement of the cathode oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is necessary to promote higher electron transport acceleration [13]. It might causes increased microbial sulfate reduction reaction and enhanced MFC performance. Another limitation for large-scale usage was attributed to the low efficiently operating cathode in neutral media [14].

The aim of current report is to enhance MFC performance at laboratory scale by creating a synergistic cooperation between the electroactive SRB biofilm in anodic chamber and improved acceleration of electrons in the cathode chamber at neutral pH. An improved acceleration could be achieved by electrochemical reduction of oxygen to water by environmental friendly catalysts as N-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped C-dots) chelated with metal ion [15]. C-dots demonstrated unique electrocatalytic, electro-optical and photocatalytic properties, low toxicity, chemical stability and abundant functional groups on their surface for chemical modifications [16]. Their semiconductive properties might be tuned by nitrogen doping in order to increase the electron density near the fermi level making them suitable catalysis for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [17]. In such a way N-doped C-dots exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity as a cathodic electrode toward ORR in both basic and acid media (0.1 M KOH and 0.1 M H2SO4, respectively) but not at neutral pH. On the other hand, the nitrogen atom in amino-groups has a comparable size with the carbon atoms and unshared electron pair [18]. Thus it can be involve in diazo-reaction with the graphite surface and/or some transition and heavy metals can be incorporated into the nitrogen-carbon matrix in the nanoparticles inner core aromatic structure [19]. The formed metal chelate complexes improve the overall efficiency of ORR activity [20]. In our report the ORR activity of the cathode electrode was significantly improved (i.e. increased achievable ORR peak current) at neutral pH by the following steps: (i) chemical coating of the hydrophobic graphite surface with N-doped C-dots via diazo-bond, and (ii) subsequent forming of metal chelate complex between the graphite phase and the organic nanolayer.

The chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of analytical grade. Citric acid, NaNO2, HCl, NaHCO3, K3[Fe(CN)6], DMF, FeCl2, CuCl2, RuCl3, NaOH and H2SO4 were purchased from Wako. Bare graphite rods (model Uni 0.5 Mm Hb Nano Dia Blended Hi-quality Mechanical Pencil Leads, made in Japan) with 1.2 nm diameter were used as working electrodes in the three-electrode cyclic voltammetry measurement, and bigger graphite rods (8 mm thickness and 90 mm length) were used as electrodes in MFC tests. The precursor and buffer solutions for electrochemical measurements of ORR were prepared with deionized ultrapure water or MilliQ water (18.0 M 18.2MΩ⋅cm, 25 °C, Water Purifying System). Size-selective desalting column (PD-10 desalting column, GE Healthcare Life Sciences) was used for separation of the synthesized nanoparticles from the mixed solution contained also salts and byproducts. Proton exchange membrane (CMI-7000S, Membrane International Inc.) was used for the design of MFC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped C-dots were prepared by microwave assisted pyrolysis known as “bottom up” method [21]. For that purpose, 1 g of citric acid was mixed with 10 ml MilliQ water and 1,2-ethylenediamine. After completely dissolving of the precursors, the light yellow transparent solution was subjected to microwave pyrolysis in 150 ml Beher glass in a microwave oven at 600 W for 180 s. After the proceeding of microwave-assisted pyrolysis a yellow-brown gum was formed at the bottom of the glass vessel. The gum was dissolved by sonication in 2 ml MilliQ water (pH = 3.5) and the ultra-small nanoparticles were separated from the byproducts by size-selective desalting column. The purity of separated nanoparticle fraction was evaluated by HPLC analysis. The optical properties were determined by UV-VIS, PL and quantum yield (QY) measurements. The nanoparticle size distribution was characterized by AFM, DLS and 200 kV TEM observation. The obtained final aqueous C-dots solution was evaporated and dried under air at 80 °C. The obtained solid resin was kept in the refrigerator within 2–3 weeks and during this period of time there was not detected any changes of the physicochemical and optical properties of the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of an electrocatalytic cathode coated with N-doped C-dots chelated with metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of electrocatalytic graphite electrode coated with metal chelate of N-doped C-dots was done within 3 stages: (i) diazotization of N-doped C-dots; (ii) chemical coating of the graphite electrode with N-doped C-dots; and (iii) formation of a metal chelate phase between the graphite surface and organic nanolayer.

Stage I – diazotization of N-doped C-dots. 500 mg purified ultra-small nanoparticles were dissolved in 10 ml MilliQ water (solution I) and cold down in ice bath. In a second vessel was prepared nitrous acid by mixing of ice, NaNO2 and conc. HCl at temperature close to 0 °C. The freshly prepared blue HNO2 (solution II) was used immediately. Thus, 2 ml of solution II was added into 10 ml of solution I and mixed for about 5 min to obtain diazonium salt. The end of the diazotization reaction was confirmed by disappearing of C-dots fluorescence under irradiation with UV lamp (365 nm excitation). The activated nanoparticles [C-dot-N≡N+]Cl– were obtained in mixed aqueous solution and immediately used for the next stage without any preliminary purification.

Stage II – chemical coating of the graphite electrode with C-dots. The surface of graphite rods was clean up careful with filter paper before performing of the chemical coating with nanoparticles. The cleaned electrodes were placed into a saturated NaHCO3 solution with pH = 8.5. The solution was continuously mixed with slow speed by magnetic stirrer and the suspension of activated [C-dot-N≡N+]Cl– nanoparticles was added dropwise. As a result, the reaction of chemical conjugation proceeded (electrode coating) followed with energetic forming of bubbles occurred on the surface of graphite electrodes (Figure S1). During the reaction of nanoparticles phase transfer from the aqueous phase to the graphite surface the color of mixed solution was smoothly bleached. The reaction kinetic and the endpoint were monitored by absorbance spectrophotometer and TLC chromatography. The nanoparticles saturation on the graphite surface was achieved for less than 30 min. The performed conjugation reaction between [C-dot-N≡N+]Cl– and graphite rods was proved by ninhydrin test [22].

Stage III – formation of a metal chelate phase between the graphite surface and organic nanolayer. The electro-catalytic phase between graphite surface and C-dots nanolayer was obtained by reaction of metal chelate formation in ethylene glycol at high temperature. First, the coated with C-dots electrodes were washed with ultrapure Milli Q water and dried at room temperature. Second, the dried electrodes were placed into a triple flask equipped with a reflux condenser and thermometer. 300 ml ethylene glycol was poured into the flask and 2 ml of ethylene glycol solution with one of the presented metal chloride salt (FeCl2, CuCl2, RuCl3 etc.) was injected. The reaction mixture was heated under reflux and Ar atmosphere at temperature around 160 °C for 4–5 h. At the end of the process the color of the reaction mixture was turned from dark to light yellow. The unreacted salt precursor was precipitated in the glass bottom. The coated electrodes were washed carefully with ethanol and water, dried and used for the next electrochemical measurements.

The modified graphite electrodes were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) using a computer-controlled potentiostat/galvanostat (Automatic Polarization System HZ-700, Hokuto Denko, Model № HAG123mP), which was is equipped with LCD monitor and includes data memory function. The measurements were conducted by a three-electrode system, including bare and modified graphite rods as working electrode (WE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode (RE) and a platinum wire (1 mm in diameter) as a counter electrode (CE). The applied scan rate was 20 mVs–1 within potential scan range of – 1.0 to 1.5 V. The electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 7), ambient temperature and in both nitrogen and oxygen-saturated solutions (obtained by bubbling for 30 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of laboratory-scale proton exchange MFC with enhanced performance
ce:section.ce:para: The microbial fuel cell was designed with two different in volume chambers – 550 ml anode and 80 ml cathode compartment. They were separated by a proton exchange membrane. A bare graphite electrode with 8 mm thickness and 90 mm length was inserted in the anode chamber. It was in contact with the SRB microorganisms, so its surface must be cleaned up. In the cathode chamber was applied equal in size graphite electrode but coated with electrocatalytic nanolayer. 60% of the volume in the anode chamber was filled with modified zeolite (particle size distribution between 2.5–5.0 mm) with the following chemical composition: 67.96% SiO2, 11.23% Al2O3, 0.83% Fe2O3, 2.85% K2O, 0.74% Na2O, 3.01 CaO, 0.06% MgO, 0.90 TiO2. Before inoculation step the zeolite was saturated with NH4Cl and KH2PO4 as biogenic important macro-elements. Culture medium of Postgate (0.5 g/L NH4Cl, 4.0 g/l MgSO4.7H2O, 2.0 g/L Na2SO4, 0.25 g/L K2HPO4, 0.1 g/L CaCl2, 6.0 g/L sodium lactate, 0.25 g/L yeast extract) with pH = 6.5 was used for cultivation of the inoculated SRB in anode chamber [23]. The concentration of SO4 2– ions in the medium was 3 g/L and the proportion between organic carbon and terminal electron acceptor was 0.67. The cathode chamber was filled up with 100 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in phosphate buffer with neutral pH = 7.01 and aerated in order to ensure oxygen saturation. pH, Eh and mV were measured in the key points of the laboratory-scale installation of MFC. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) was measured with a portable digital multimeter (Keithley Model-175). A precise potentiometer with maximum value of 11.5 kΩ was used for variation of external resistances.

The morphology and nanoparticle size distribution were determined by ImageJ software [24] directly from the micrographic images obtained by FEI tecnai G2 20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 120 kV accelerating voltage. To prepare the samples for TEM observations, the nanoparticle suspensions were diluted with ultrapure water and one droplet (volume = 1 μL) was dropped onto the carbon film (15 nm in thickness) on copper grid (400 × 100 mesh, Agar Scientific, UK). The grids were preliminary discharged by ion coater (IB-2, EikoEngineering, Co., Ltd. Japan). The sample was dried in vacuum at room temperature for 5 min before TEM observation. For the atomic force microscopic (AFM) measurements the nanoparticles were first immobilized on mica film in order to form a homogeneous monolayer [25]. A home-built AFM with a commercial SPM controller (Asylum Research, ARC2) was applied to obtain a topographical image and height-profile analysis of the immobilized C-dots monolayer. The used Si cantilever had a resonance frequency of 300 kHz and a spring of 40 N/m (Olympus AC 160). The surface chemistry analysis of N-doped C-dots was carried out by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using a Bruker FT-IT spectrometer TENSOR II equipped with Platinum ATR. Spectrometer, and by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The absorbance spectra of the purified nanoparticles were obtained by UV-VIS Jasco analytical spectrophotometer (model V-570) and the photoluminescence by Jasco analytical photoluminescence spectrophotometer (model FP-6300). The quantum yield (QY) was acquired by the absolute PL QY measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, model C9920-03 G). Raman spectra were measured with a Laser Raman WiTec Alpha 300 spectrometer using the green exciting radiation (532 nm) from an Ar+ ion laser. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature.

The paracrystalline nature of the nanoparticles proposed in the illustrated chemical structure was confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2A). The first order Raman spectra revealed two relatively broad bands centered at ˜1360 cm–1 and ˜ 1570 cm–1 with certain overlapping. The bands were denoted as D band (assigned to the structural disorder and amorphous sp3-hybridized carbon) and G band (typical of graphitic order, in this case assigned to the polyaromatic carbon domains in carbogenic core). The solution of the dissolved N-doped C-dots has yellow visible color with a broad UV-VIS absorption below 600 nm (Fig. 2B). The absorption spectrum has two peaks at 236 nm (assigned π–π* aromatic transitions of the sp2-hybridized domains in the carbogenic core) and at 360 nm (attributed to n–π* transition of chromophore CN and CO groups) with a tail extended into the visible range. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum is roughly symmetrical with maximum emission peak 448 nm (excitation wavelength = 360 nm). The aqueous solution of the nanoparticles always shows excitation-dependent PL emission, and emits blue luminescence color when it is excited by commercial UV-lamp with 365 nm irradiation wavelength. Its measured PL quantum yield was ˜ 56%, which is close to those of the inorganic semiconductive quantum dots. The quantum yield of the solution exhibits robust stability and without any change or noticeable precipitation of the nanoparticles in dark during 3 months of observations. This was confirmed also by the results of PL and TEM. FTIR spectra of N-doped C-dots was employed to investigate their surface and functional groups (Fig. 2C). We confirmed the presence of an intense broad absorption bands between 3500–2500 cm–1 which are characteristic for ν(OH), ν(NH) stretching vibrations [31] and ν(CH) symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations (2925–2855 cm–1) respectively. The strong signal at 1650 cm–1 indicates the presence of sp2-hybridized lattice domains (CC stretching). The band at 1700 cm–1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of ν(CO) groups. The characteristic stretching vibrations of ν(CN), ν(COC) and bending vibration of ν(CO) bond can be observed at 1445 cm–1, 1300 cm–1 and 1387 / 1058 cm–1, respectively. XPS data (Fig. 2D) illustrate that the nanoparticles are composed mainly of carbon (˜ 70%), oxygen (˜ 25%) and nitrogen (< 2%) elements. The peak C1s at 287 eV is due to the presence of CC, CN structures and oxygen-containing groups as CO and CO. The O1s peak at 532 eV is associated to oxygen atoms bonded to carbon trough sp2-orbitals (aromatic and carboxyl groups). The peak N1s at 403 eV is attributed to nitrogen intercalated in terminal or bridge position of the carbon network [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of eletrocatalytic cathode as a working electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of aromatic amino-groups on the nanoparticle surface (as it was proved above) gives us opportunity to employ diazonium coupling reaction for chemical conjugation of C-dots to the surface of graphite electrode (Fig. 3 ). This reaction was chosen because electrons can be delocalize through the diazo coupling up to C-dot. At the first step N-doped C-dots were converted to diazonium salts by simple treating with mix of sodium nitrite and hydrochloric acid in ice bath. The successful proceed of reaction was accompanied with quenching of the nanoparticle fluorescence. At the first step, the obtained diazonium chloride salt [C-dots-N≡N+] Cl– react as an efficient electrophile on the graphite surface at room temperature and weak alkaline pH of solution. During the conjugation reaction the released hydrochloride was neutralized by the sodium carbonate and bubbles were generated on the electrodes surface as shown on Fig. S1. The kinetic reaction of mass phase transfer was monitored by measuring the absorbance intensity of the mixed solution. After saturation of the coated nanoparticles on the graphite surface (for about 30 min), i.e. the endpoint of conjugation reaction the electrodes were carefully washed and analyzed with nynhidrin test and cyclic voltammetry. Heating of the coated graphite electrode in aqueous solution of nynhidrin gives the characteristic Ruhemann's purple color as it is shown on Fig. S2, which is qualitative analysis of the immobilized N-doped C-dots. The cyclic voltammetry analysis is discussed below. At the second step, chelation between a metal ion, polydentate nanoparticle surface and diazo-bond occurred. In such a way an electrocatalytic phase was formed between the conjugated nanolayer and the graphite surface. The central ion (Me2+) might be Fe2+, Cu2+, Ru2+, etc., and it is bonded through the nitrogen atoms (NN) and the abundant presence of other coordinated atoms (oxygen, nitrogen) on C-dots surface. This complex reaction occurred in ethylene glycol and the use of C-dots coating was a key toward fabrication of electrocatalytic cathode.

In order to prove the formation of the diazo-bond and the metal chelate complex we accomplished similar coupling reaction experiment but the graphite rod was exchanged with the organic dye fluorescein [25]. The combination approach of Abs, PL and FRET spectral data as shown on Fig. 4 enabled systematic analysis and monitoring of all proceeded reactions used for electrode fabrication. The absorbance spectra of bare C-dots, nanoparticles as well as coupled with fluorescein nanoparticles (CDs–N = N–Ar) and metal chelate complex (Fig. 4A) verified the successful performing of conjugation reaction. The diazotization coupling in solution was observed even by naked eyes since that color of reaction mixture turned from light yellow to bright red. The purified C-dots–N = N–Ar nanoparticles exhibit the peaks correspond to n–π* transition at 360 nm assigned to N-doped C-dots and the characteristic intense peak of fluorescein dye at 490 nm, respectively. The typical absorption edge for azo-group (in the range between 350–370 nm) was not identified on the absorbance spectra, because of the tautomerism existence in the aqueous sample [33]. The formation of chelate complex caused a weak hypsochromic shift (˜ 20 nm) of the absorbance edge belonging to the n–π* transition of C-dots. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (Fig. 4B) were used for more detailed analysis of the metal chelate formation. The bare C-dots possess strong blue PL emission due to their quantum-sized graphite structure (black solid line). After diazotization their emission signal was quenched, because the obtained azo compound (CDs–N = N–Ar) undergo to photoisomerization. Its photo-excited state is accompanied with the change of direction of the nitrogen lone pair, i.e. rotation around nitrogen-nitrogen bond and the observed emission color was green (green solid line) [25]. However, the formation of metal chelate complex with the participation of the azo-bond causes restraining of the lone pair and prevention of the photoisomerization. Thus the emission belonged to the quantum-sized graphite structure of C-dots was recovered and we observed one PL signal as a sum of the shared emissions between the nanoparticle and organic dye (red solid line). The sharing-resulted PL signal of (CDs–N = N–Ar)Me2+ complex caused as whole bathochromic shift of PL and observed emission color was yellow. By FTIR spectroscopic analysis it was difficult to study the metal chelate complex but the existence of the azo-bond was well confirmed. As it is shown on Fig. 4C the spectra of bare C-dots, azo-modified (CDs–N = N–Ar) and metal chelate (CDs–N = N–Ar)Me2+ complex are plotted together. The characteristic absorption band for diazo (-N = N-) functional group occurred at 1580 cm–1 (pointed with an arrow) and it proves the successful proceeding of coupling reaction. There was no any other significant difference between the spectra and the other absorption bands were explained above. At the bottom of the figure are shown images of the emission color of bare and conjugated nanoparticles under irradiation with UV lamp. We identified the change of visual fluorescence of bare C-dots (blue), fluorescein azo-C-dot (green) and metal chelate complex (yellow color).

The electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrodes as cathodes for ORR in neutral phosphate solution is demonstrated on Fig. 5 . Although N-doped C-dots show outstanding electrocatalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline solution (with cathodic peaks at – 0.71 V and – 0.56 V at 0.1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH, respectively) [34], a distinguishable cathodic peak cannot be observed in neutral oxygen-saturated solution as shown in our experiment (violet solid line). As a result the metal free C-dots showing no electro-catalytic activity as same as the control experiment with bare graphite rod (solid gray line). Apparently, the introduction of metal-ion phase through formation of chelate complex (as shown on the scheme on Fig. 3) caused electrocatalytic performance of the electrode and a large cathodic ORR peak in O2 saturated neutral electrolyte. On Fig. 5A the ORR currents are compared among the cathodic traces of electrocatalytic current containing with Cu2+ (solid blue line), Fe2+ (solid brown line) and Ru2+ (solid red line) as well as carbon-supported Pt catalyst (solid black line) with 20 wt% metal loading (or 20% Pt/Carbon) as a control experiment. The electrode potential was depicted with respect to saturated calomel electrode (SCE) [35]. The cathodic peaks for Cu2+, Fe2+ and Ru2+ were observed respectively at –0.38 V, –0.29 V and –0.18 V. These values indicated anodic shifts of 0.33 V, 0.42 V and 0.89 V respectively, in comparison with the acidic cathodic peak of bear N-doped C-dots. Their measured onset potentials were at – 0.16 V, – 0.15 V and 0.16 V. The data for transition d-metals successfully demonstrated the promising possibility for their potential commercial application as cathodic electrodes in fuel cell technology. For comparison, CV curves of the control experiment with 20 wt% carbon-supported Pt show typical cathodic peak at – 0.12 V and onset at 0.06 V. The cathodic trace data of Ru-nanolayer were better than those of Pt/Carbon catalyst used as a control experiment (Pt peak at – 0.13 V and onset at 0.07 V). The electrode potential at the cathodic peak current of Ru2+ was shifted with – 0.05 V from those of Pt, however its potential onset was anodically shifted with 0.10 V. At the Ru2+ containing C-dot modified electrode, the sharp increase of ORR current started at more positive potential demonstrate lowest over potential and fast kinetics of the electron transfer process, which is of highly importance for its application in MFC. In addition, the chelate complex of Ru2+ was much more resistance toward poisoning compared with the poison sensitive platinum surface. Nevertheless, the study of the anodic trace (as shown on Fig. S3) show that Ru2+ was oxidized and dissolved into the electrolyte solution. After that ORR catalytic activity disappeared. Even though, the cathodic peak was characterized with a remarkably good stability of the reduction reaction in oxygen saturated solution (solid red line) as shown on Fig. 5B. In the case of the saturated with nitrogen solution (solid red line) there was not significant oxygen evolution. In both cases we registered ORR activity, which did not exist at neutral pH with metal free C-dots (violet solid line).

The redox potential of Ru2+ is strongly dependent on the chelate ligands (L). The possible simple ORR steps in the potential window between 0.1 ˜ 0.2 V are:
Ru2+L → Ru1+L + O2 → Ru2+L + O2–
The reduction potential of Ru(2+/1+) is – 1.2 V. Then, O2 might be trapped at Ru, which navigate electron through the conjugated bridge.

The further experiment in MFC were performed with a graphite cathode modified with C-dots co-doped with Ru2+ due to the excellent electrocatalytic properties of coated nanofilm to reduce oxygen at neutral pH of the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploitation of the electrocatalytic cathode in MFC based on sulfate-reducing bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The used design of a laboratory-scale MFC with dual-chamber is shown on Fig. 6 . The scheme of general principle for operation (Fig. 6A) includes an air-breathing cathode chamber with working electrode and an anoxic anode chamber with electroactive biofilm. SRB were inoculated into the anode chamber and the microbial population was kept to grow in few days until MFC reaching of continuous maximum current density generation (˜ 143 ± 2.4 mA/m2). During the experiment the anode chamber was fed from a vessel with fresh nutrition medium (culture medium of Postgate) [10]. The cathode and anode chambers were divided through proton-selective membrane. It is permeable only for the released H+ generated from microbial produced H2S in the anodic chamber. Approximately half of the volume of the anodic chamber was filled with zeolite, which play role as a bearer and reservoir of the biofilm as shown on the image on Fig. 6B. Colonies of rod-shaped sulfate-reducing bacteria were formed directly on the solid graphite surface (as shown on SEM micrograph in Fig. 6C) that later build electroactive biofilm. In the established environment SRB continuously formed denser biofilm on the anode electrode, as well as in the zeolite reservoir. Direct electron transfer to the anode electrode occur effectively from SRB in the biofilm through electrically-conductive network facilitated by filaments known as nano-pili, which connect the individual cells [6]. In our experiments the thicker biofilm at pH between 7˜8.5 enhanced the anode performance, which caused gradual increase in current production. The open circuit voltage (OCV) was in linear dependence on the concentration of the biogenic produced hydrogen sulphide in anodic zone [10]. Thus the microorganisms were utilized as a self-regenerable catalyst. The used mesophilic SRB are most productive at 37 °C, and therefore, the temperature in all measurements was fixed at this optimal value. Since the electrochemical processes of transferring of electrons derived from bacterial enzyme oxidation to the solid electrode are well known [36], our efforts were concentrated to investigate the synergistic effect between electroactive biofilm and electrocatalytic nanolayer.

The output performance characteristics were obtained on MFC after SRB attained the stable OCV. Using periodically increase of the load, the voltage was measured and the current was calculated using Ohms law as shown on Fig. 7 . As mentioned above the released protons in the anodic chamber are migrating into the cathode side, where they are converted into water in coupled ORR reaction. Thus the overall redox reaction is completed on the cathode electrode. However, the graphite cathode is not able to fully exploit oxygen because of large overpotential, which is resulted in the slow ORR [37]. These losses were minimize and the cathodic current density was increased by introducing of electrocatalytic metal doped C-dots nanolayer on the graphite, which accelerated the electron transport. On Fig. 7A is shown the power curve that describes the power density obtained on bare graphite cathode (blue line) and the cathode coated with electrocatalytic nanolayer (red line) as a function of applied current density. In case of electrocatalytic nanolayer at the maximum power density of 59 mW/m2 it was measured current density of 196 mA/m2 (red arrow). Without nanolayer coating, i.e. based only on the electroactive biofilm, the maximum current density of 143 mA/m2 was measured at power density 33 mW/m2 (blue arrow). The obtained result accounted almost double higher enhancement of the power density (from 33 to 59 mW/m2) due to the synergistic effect between biofilm and C-dots chelated with metal ions. In case of the polarization curve (Fig. 7B) we found a similar effect, i.e. the voltage of the bare graphite cathode (blue line) and the cathode coated with electrocatalytic nanolayer (red line) is represented as a function of the current density. The measured voltage was significantly higher (300 mV, red arrow) in respect of the synergistic effect than the simple biofilm operation (230 mV, blue arrow).

The presented polarization curves did not suffer from diffusion loss. This was an indication that the occurred losses in MFC are only due to ohmic losses. The reported experimental data with nano coated graphite cathode were reproducible within 5–6 h exploitation life of MFC.

In this study we found that the synergistic cooperation between SRB electroactive biofilm and electrocatalytic N-doped C-dots chelated with metal ion caused an enhancement of the current production in MFC. One of the reason for increased current density was the acceleration of ORR and electron transportation on the cathode surface thanks to the immobilized carbon nanodots and their metal-chelate complexes with the graphite surface. Despite the best MFC data obtained with ruthenium our results showed that the incorporation of other earth-abundant metal ions (iron, copper, etc.) in C-dots nanolayer caused significant enhancement of ORR catalytic activity of the cathode. The chemical resistance and exploitation life of fabricated electrodes are opening up the possibility for new class cheap electrocatalysts. The improved cathodic reaction kinetics as well as the reproducibility of the bioelectrochemical system enables their practical application in variety ecotechnologies for simultaneous treatment of environmental pollutions and energy production at mild operating conditions.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Saitama University for the financial support of this investigation. The study was supported also by Grant № DN07/7, 15.12.2016 funded from the Bulgarian National Science Fund. This study was accomplished within the bilateral agreement between Saitama University – Japan and the University of Mining and Geology “St. Ivan Rilski” – Bulgaria.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2019.04.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Diazotization of bare graphite electrodes, Ninhidrin reaction of C-dots coated graphite electrodes, Cyclic voltammograms of graphite electrodes coated with Ru-doped C-dots in buffer solutions saturated to oxygen and nitrogen.
